content.assessment: This proposal directly engages a documented precondition problem: reform-sympathetic experts have identified that structural selection cannot be eliminated without first addressing disparities in school quality, since family demand for selective tracks stems from distrust in local schools. This gives the sequencing logic direct evidential support, but capacity to deliver quality improvements is constrained by the same structural teacher shortage and low-wage conditions affecting the whole system, meaning the proposal's precondition-fixing mechanism faces its own capacity bottleneck.
opportunities: Could be sequenced as a necessary first phase before any track-structure reform, using the precondition logic to justify a monitored, staged investment plan.
strengths: Directly addresses a sequencing/precondition problem explicitly identified by reform-sympathetic experts: selection cannot be eliminated without first fixing underlying school-quality disparities.
Focuses on a root driver (distrust in local school quality) rather than a downstream symptom, which is coherent with implementation logic of addressing preconditions before structural change.
threats: Centralized decision-making (post-2013) has already shown tension and failure to resolve structural funding challenges, raising risk that centrally-directed quality investment faces the same implementation friction.
weaknesses: The same structural teacher shortage and low-wage conditions constraining the whole system also constrain the capacity needed to deliver quality improvements in comprehensive schools specifically.
